hub_subtitle: Conversação para viagens, listening de verdade e reuniões internacionais — rumo a Nova York, setembro de 2026

lesson.menu_title: Eating Out with Confidence -- Restaurants, Orders, and Recommendations
lesson.menu_desc: Da reserva a conta em um restaurante de Nova York: como recomendar, sugerir e pedir -- I'd recommend, you might want to try, why don't you, should I -- 39 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 8 -- Eating Out with Confidence
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucimara | Lesson 8 -- Eating Out with Confidence

listenings.text: Good evening, Ms. Ito, this is Diane calling from Bistro Sixty in Midtown, confirming your reservation for two at eight o'clock this Thursday. We are looking forward to having you. I have a few notes on your table, and I wanted to go over them before you arrive. First, I see that one of your guests is gluten-free. Thank you for telling us in advance. That was the right thing to do, because our chef can prepare almost the whole menu without gluten, but he needs a little time to plan it. So when you sit down, you don't have to explain it all again. Just remind your server, and the kitchen will already know. Second, a small suggestion. If it is a special dinner, you might want to try the tasting menu. It is five courses, and the chef chooses each one, so you don't have to read the whole menu or decide anything. I'd recommend it for a business dinner, because it lets you talk instead of order. And last, one nice thing. The first plate of the evening, the warm bread and the olive oil, is always complimentary at your table. See you Thursday, Ms. Ito. And if anything changes, please call us back.

Welcome back to The Confident Diner. Today, the one thing that separates a nervous tourist from someone who looks like they eat out in America every week. And it is not the food words. It is a single question. When the server comes over and the menu is a wall of words you half understand, most people freeze and point at something. Don't. Ask the question that a local always asks: what do you recommend? Four words, and suddenly the server is on your side, doing the hard part for you. And listen to how they answer, because it is never a full sentence. It is I'd go with the salmon, or you might wanna try the special, or honestly, the short rib is amazing tonight. Those little openers, I'd go with, you might wanna, why don't you, that is the whole grammar of a recommendation, and once you hear them, you hear them everywhere. Now, two things nobody tells you. One, in America the tip is not on the bill. You look at the tab, and you add eighteen to twenty percent yourself, on top. Nobody reminds you, and if you forget, it is not a small thing here. Two, when you host, you say three words at the end and you say them early, before anyone reaches for a wallet. It's on me. Say it with a smile, and the dinner is yours.

reading.rtitle: A Table for Two in Midtown
paras: The reservation was for eight o'clock, and Lucimara arrived first, on purpose. She wanted five quiet minutes with the menu before her guest sat down. Mr. Chen was flying in from Shanghai for two days, and this dinner mattered more to her than any meeting on the trip. For years, at tables exactly like this one, she had let other people order for her, because the menu was a wall of words and she did not want to get it wrong in front of anyone. Tonight she wanted to be the one who looked up and said, "I'd recommend the sea bass," and meant it.

The server, Marco, brought two menus and a small plate of warm bread. "This is complimentary," he said, "on the house." Lucimara opened the menu and, for the first time, she could read the shape of it: the appetizers on the left, the entrees down the middle, and a short list of side dishes at the bottom. She still did not know every word, but she knew where each thing lived. When Mr. Chen finally sat down, she did not hand him the menu in silence. "Why don't you start with the soup?" she said. "And you might want to try the sea bass. Marco says it's the best thing on the menu tonight."

There was one thing she had to get right. Mr. Chen had a dietary restriction: he could not eat gluten. So before anyone ordered, she turned to Marco. "He's gluten-free. Should I mention it to the kitchen now, or later?" Now, Marco told her, always now. It was the right question, and better asked early than sent back to the kitchen half-cooked. For herself, she ordered the steak, medium-rare, and a side dish of grilled vegetables. When the sommelier came over with a suggestion of his own, "I'd recommend a light red with the sea bass," she did not simply nod the way she always had. "Is it anything like a Pinot Noir?" she asked. It was.

At the end came the part she had always dreaded, and it was never the food, it was the paying. The tab arrived in a small leather folder. In the United States, the tip is not printed on the bill; you read the total, and you add eighteen to twenty percent yourself. She knew the tipping etiquette now, and she added twenty without a second of doubt. "It's on me," she told Mr. Chen, before he could reach for his wallet. He smiled and thanked her in careful English. Two hours, one language, and no help from anyone. The supplier from Shanghai had just watched a director from São Paulo host a whole dinner in English, from the first recommendation to the last.

gist.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: New York restaurants are expensive, and foreigners should avoid the fancy ones.
Hosting a dinner is not about knowing every word on the menu. It is about being the one who recommends, asks the right question, and takes care of the table.
Mr. Chen could not find anything to eat because he was gluten-free.

items: Marco charged them extra for the warm bread and the olive oil.
"'This is complimentary,' he said, 'on the house.'" Complimentary means it is free -- the restaurant does not charge for it.
Lucimara recommended the sea bass to her guest instead of staying silent.
"She did not hand him the menu in silence... 'you might want to try the sea bass. Marco says it's the best thing on the menu tonight.'"
Mr. Chen was able to eat anything on the menu.
"Mr. Chen had a dietary restriction: he could not eat gluten." A dietary restriction is a food he cannot or must not eat.
Lucimara asked the kitchen about the gluten before they ordered, not after.
"'Should I mention it to the kitchen now, or later?' Now, Marco told her, always now... better asked early than sent back to the kitchen half-cooked."
In the United States, the tip was already printed on the bill.
"the tip is not printed on the bill; you read the total, and you add eighteen to twenty percent yourself." That is the tipping etiquette she had to learn.

items.situation: The server hands you a menu full of words you half understand. Do not point. Ask the one question a local always asks.
tips: What would you recommend? It's my first time here.
This question puts the server on your side -- and the answer is usually one short phrase you can follow.

items.situation: Your guest is looking at the menu, unsure. Suggest the fish -- gently, not as an order.
tips: You might want to try the sea bass. Marco says it's the best thing tonight.
'You might want to try...' + base verb. It is a suggestion, so it leaves your guest free to say no.

items.situation: Your business partner cannot eat gluten. Check with the kitchen -- and ask BEFORE you order, not after.
tips: He's gluten-free. Should I mention it to the kitchen before we order?
'Should I...?' asks for advice. Ask early -- a dietary restriction is easier to plan than to fix on the plate.

items.situation: You want the steak, but not well-done. Tell the server exactly how you want it cooked.
tips: I'll go with the steak, medium-rare, please. And could I get a side of vegetables?
'I'll go with...' is how you place your order. 'Could I get...?' is the review request -- polite, from Lesson 7.

items.situation: The sommelier is at the table and you have no idea about the wine. Turn it into a question you can follow.
tips: What would you recommend with the sea bass? Is it anything like a Pinot Noir?
Ask for a recommendation, then compare it to something you know. Now you are steering the conversation, not drowning in it.

items.situation: The tab arrives in a leather folder. Your Chinese supplier reaches for his wallet. Stop him -- warmly, and fast.
tips: Please, it's on me. And don't worry about the tip -- I've got it.
'It's on me' means you are paying. Say it early, before the folder even opens, and remember: in the U.S. you add the tip yourself.
